steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a dangling modifier question. The sentence opens with a participial phrase — “Woven from recycled yarn and hand tufted using a carpet weaving technique passed down by the artist’s Turkish grandmother” — which must modify the subject of the independent clause that follows. The decisive clue: the things that were woven and hand-tufted are the tapestries, not the artist herself.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the topological tapestries of Argentine textile artist Alexandra Kehayoglou are: the subject is the tapestries, so the participial phrase correctly modifies them; the resulting clause is grammatical and logical. ✓
  • Choice B — the Argentine textile artist Alexandra Kehayoglou creates topological tapestries that are: the subject is Kehayoglou, not the tapestries — the participial phrase dangles because the artist was not woven or hand-tufted. ✗
  • Choice C — when she creates her topological tapestries, Argentine textile artist Alexandra Kehayoglou makes them: the subject is again Kehayoglou, causing the same dangling modifier error; “when she creates” also introduces a second adverbial clause that disrupts the sentence structure. ✗
  • Choice D — Alexandra Kehayoglou is an Argentine textile artist whose topological tapestries are: the subject is Kehayoglou — the participial phrase dangles because she, not her tapestries, is the grammatical subject following the opening modifier. ✗
That is the correct answer.
